paragraphs.text: No one who examines a map of
can fail to remark how singularly few are the islands lying along
boundary line, there are but two groups of islands,
those of the Santa Barbara channel
[.] Not only are these the only two groupes [sic], but the islands forming them are all that
can claim. Of these two groups the
are really the only ones that deserve the name,
being but a collection of rocky islets. Notwithstanding this fact
is to most people a terra incognita, yet neither its history nor its description is devoid of interest, as it is believed the present article will show.
First, then, as to the history of the
is of the opinion as are others that they were first discovered by
,] the Spanish navigator. In 1542, an opinion with which
coincides, although he doubts whether the date of discovery usually given (September, 1542) does not reall[y] […]er to
) October 3d [3rd] [he sailed] three days, or about eightee[n] leagues, along a c[oas]t of valley [and] plain[s] and s[m]okes, with high mountains in the interior, until he came to
some seven leagues out[.] To the two largest islands he gave the names o[f] two of his vessels, the
, names which were afterward respectively supplanted by those of
. Both these islands appeared to be thickly populated,
showing themselves by turns timid and hostile. The Spaniards, however, appeased them by signs, and, some of them venturing off in a canoe to receive gifts,
returned with them and landed on
on or about October 6, 1542. Next day they crowded over to
, and after remaining there four days proceeded further up the channel. On the 13th they anchored off the islands of
, and then, on the 1[5]th, they sailed past
, naming it San Lucas, and so passed out of
islands of the Santa Barbara channel
, "except a uniform exaggeration in the size of
, and confusion in locating them, agrees very well with the truth as revealed by later mission annals, and by the relics exhumed in late years by antiquarians.
The region was certainly inhabited in early times by
who used canoes, lived main[l]y by fishing and were much superior in many respects to most other natives of California.
There was a tendency at first," he adds, "as is usual in such cases, to ascribe the channel relies [relics] to a prehistoric race, but nothing indicating such an origin has ever been found there."
After pushing north as far as
and his fleet returned southward, and on the 23rd of November re-entering the channel from the north discovered
, which they named Posesion. Here they found a good harbor, now known as
being ill from the effects of a fall received in October and subsequent exposure, it was determined to winter there.
died January 3, 1543, and was buried on
near the harbor shore. No trace of his last resting place has, however, been found, and the drifting sands have perhaps made such a discovery doubtful.

was a Portuguese navigator in the Spanish service, of whom nothing is known beyond the skill and bravery displayed on this expedition, and the fact that his reputation was believed to justify his appointment as commander. As the burial place of
, then, have at least one claim to historical value, while to this bold mariner,
, the first to discover her coasts, if to any one,
might with propriety erect a monument.
It may be added that numerous unsuccessful searches have been made to discover Cabrillo's grave. In 1875 Messrs.
found the pits on a level near
, about ten feet in diameter, which they believed were the resting places of
and some of his men, but no bones were found. Everything, indeed, seems to have conspired to efface the memory of
. When dying he gave over the command to
, his Levantine pilato mayor, and Ferraio's first act was to change the name of
from Posesion to Juan Rodrigues in honor of
, but even that name has been taken away from
The man who performed this act of vandalism was that great name-changer
, who, with a Spanish exploring fleet of three vessels, anchored in
bay November 10, 1603. It had been sixty years since
, but here as elsewhere on the
paid no heed to the disoveries of his predecessor. He named everything anew, and when he reached the
, he simply expunged every previous name and set up a completely new nomenclature of his own.
played a somewhat similar trick on
in 1703, and other navigators followed the same cheerful plan of rechristening everything, so that but very few of Vizcaino's original names have remained.
[Vizcaino] only seems to have touched at
, which he describes as having a large population of fishermen and [t]raders, who had large, well-built canoes and houses. They had no fear and were friendly, though skillful thieves.
, who accompanied this expedition, gives very particular descriptions of a temple to the sun, to which they sacrificed birds, together with a quantity of images and [id]ols set up along the shore.
became well-known landmarks to those composing the various expeditions, both lay and clerical, and intercourse with
became a common thing. The inevitable result was not long delayed.
in his trading schooner in 1804 he counted the number of inhabitants at 160, and when
made his biennial report in 1805 he reported that the group had been visited by the "sarampion," or measles, which had carried off over 200 natives on two islands alone[.] Then came
on their otter-hunting expeditions, quarrels and massacres, until in 1815 the few survivors took refuge in
as the solitary relic of one of the most numerous and certainly the most interesting tribes of California Indians.
passed into the possession of favored Spaniards, and from them were purchased and variously [secured] by their present owners, who use them for grazing and sheep ranches[.]
have not proved a very rich field, although some very interesting antiquarian relics have bee[n] gathered up by
, by several [Euro]pean expeditions for their various [gov]ernments and by a number of privat[e] explorations[.] Entire skeletons of [In]dians have been found in many of the caves with which
abound[.] The signs of their rancherias to[o] were formerly very frequent and the remains of metates, mo[r]tars and various ute[n]sils quite [c]ommon.
Next, as to the description of
. Notwithstanding the many visits by navigators, the fact remains that until

first examined them nothing a[ccu]rate was known of their number[,] peculiaritie[s,] exten[t], or position[.] Upon all maps of as recent date as 1850 a mythical island [c]al[l]ed San Juan was laid down, and upon a map of
and dated 1847, there are set down no less than twelve large islands, the positions and extent of which are most grotesquely erroneous[.] As a good description of
it may be said that they are of high, bold and rugged appearance, in many places show precipitou[s] cliffs, and are at present incapable of sustaining anything except a very sparse population.

is twenty and a half miles long, with an average breadth of two miles and a circuit of fifty[.] It is high and bold, the southern end being the higher, and the land gradually [fall]ing to the northward. At the northwest end is a small indentation forming an anchorage, but it is anything but a safe and pleasant place in bad northwest weather, while even in heavy southerly weather there is a disagreeable swell. Upon the southeast end of

, a name which fully indicated its former use. The entire island is a mass of sandstone, which rises to a vast pyramid at the southeast 1500 feet high[.] The whole surface is bro[k]en by gulches, without a vestige of wood or a drop of running water.

is seventeen and one-half miles long, with an average breadth of four miles and a circuit of forty-two. It rises to a height of about three thousand feet, and is remarkable for the great transverse break or depression, running partly through it, and forming an anchorage or cove at each side[.] The land connecting these is a low spit not over thirty feet high. A wharf has been built in the cove facing

, and small craft can readily find protection from the prevailing winds. The shores elsewhere are rocky, and on the southern side fearfully abrupt, but on the northern shore there are several indentations where boats may ride at almost any season. Deep and precipitous gulches mark the island, but there are several pleasant valleys where good water has been obtained by sinking wells. Scrub oak is plentiful, and a growth of chapparel covers almost the entire island[.] It has been stocked, and several settlers live on

is a small island, having but two miles of shore line. Its elevation at the highest part is about 500 feet, and the top has an area of about thirty acres well covered with soil, but no water is found, and not a vestige of wood. The shores are rocky and abrupt, presenting perpendicular cliffs to the full force of the swell on the northeast and south.
is the most distant from the coast of all

, as well as the driest and most sterile. It is about 600 feet high, abrupt and flattopped. The sides are bold and precipitous, and composed of coarse sandstone. Its length is eight miles, with an average and nearly uniform width of three and one-half miles.

is in fact a curiously formed group of three islands, their entire length being five miles. They are composed of coarse, dark gray sandstone, very rotten and crumbling, the sides are perpendicular, running up into a number of peaks, one of which is 930 feet high. The whole formation is filled with innumerable cavities, giving it the appearance of an enormous blackened honeycomb. At the eastern end is found a very beautiful arch in one of the outlying rocks. The soil is loose and thin, producing only a few dwarfed species of cactus and a thick-leafed, succulent plant. Not a drop of water is to be found.
is a place of great resort for the seal, sea lion and formerly the otter, but the latter have been killed off. It was on
ran ashore during a dense fog at midnight on December 2, 1853, in calm weather.

, having a length of twenty-one miles, an average width of four and a circuit of not less than fifty-three. On the northern side of the island and near the middle the shore makes a moderately deep curve, forming a ro[…]dstead [roadstead] called
, at the opening of a valley, where are ple[n]ty of both wood and water.

is bold and about 1700 feet in height. Its eastern part is extremely irregular, barren and destitute of water, while the northeastern portion is thickly strewn with large angular pieces of stone, broken as if with a hammer. Several species of cactus and course grasses flourish. Small gray foxes used to abound here.
is composed of coarse, dark-gray sandstone, crumbling and rotten, like that of
is in the shape of a parallelogram, fifteen miles long and ten wide. It has two or three modestly good anchorages, but the soundings around
do not show as deep water as around the others. It is not so bold as
, but attains an elevation of 1172 feet[.] The hills are rocky and covered with coarse grass and bushes.
is the most western of the

, seven and one-half miles long, an average breadth of two and one-half miles and a circuit of twenty-one. Its western extremity is bold and narrow, gradually increasing in breadth until it attains three and one-half miles. The narrow end of the island is composed of sand dunes, therein differing from all the other islan[d]s[.] On the northeast side of
already referred to, where is good anchorage, and which was named by

is 232 miles[.] They are much visited by fogs, and have a rainy season that commences in the early part of November and continues until the middle of March, although rainstorms are not infrequent in the summer[.]
